summary: Gruber examines the controversial redesign of the Mail app toolbar in iOS 13, which replaced five discrete action buttons with just two: Trash and Reply. The change moved familiar actions like Flag, Move, and New Message behind the Reply button's action sheet, causing users to accidentally delete emails due to muscle memory. While Gruber appreciates the new action sheet design itself, he questions why Apple consolidated the toolbar so aggressively when there was room for more buttons. He argues the redesign needed a first-run explainer and that the two remaining buttons should at least be spaced apart to prevent accidental taps.
key_points: iOS 13 Mail replaced a five-button toolbar (Flag, Move, Trash, Reply, New Message) with just two buttons (Trash and Reply), putting the trash icon where reply used to be
The Reply button now functions as a 'Do Something With This Message' sheet, but using the Reply icon for this broad purpose is unintuitive and hides previously visible features
The design wastes toolbar space with unused whitespace while forcing users through extra taps for common actions like Flag, Move, and New Message
Apple failed to include any first-run guidance showing users where familiar features moved, leading to widespread confusion and accidental deletions
A simple fix would be to place Trash on the far left with whitespace between it and Reply to prevent muscle-memory mistakes
money_quotes: That new 'Reply' action sheet is really a 'Do Something With This Message' sheet — I'm not sure what the icon for this should be, but the Reply icon seems like an odd choice.
They — reasonably! — never thought to look for it by tapping what clearly looks like the old familiar Reply icon.
It's one thing to put new features (for which there's no room on the toolbar) in the Reply action sheet; it's another to move commands like Flag and Move that already had positions on the toolbar.
iOS 13 should have included a first-run explainer showing users where these features moved to.
What's the point of having only two buttons and all that unused whitespace on the left side?
At the very least, if the toolbar is only going to have these two buttons, why not place the Trash button on the far left, and put the whitespace between the two buttons?
key_insight: iOS 13 Mail's toolbar redesign sacrificed discoverability and muscle memory for a minimalist two-button layout that wastes space while hiding previously visible functionality behind a misleadingly labeled Reply button.
